title: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine
authors: Daniel B. Mark, John B. Wong

items.title: The Dual Nature of Medical Practice: Science and Uncertainty
items.content: Medical practice is characterized by both scientific knowledge and inherent uncertainty. While technical mastery is essential, it alone does not suffice due to the probabilistic nature of clinical decisions.
keyPoints: Sir William Osler described medicine as 'a science of uncertainty and an art of probability'.
Clinical practice involves irreducible uncertainty that must be acknowledged by clinicians.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · Decision-Making in Clinical Medicine

items.title: Core Pillars for Modern Medical Practice
items.content: To achieve excellence in modern medicine, physicians must develop expertise in clinical reasoning, rational diagnostic test use and interpretation, and integrate research evidence with clinical judgment.
keyPoints: Three pillars of modern medical practice: (1) Clinical reasoning; (2) Rational diagnostic test use and interpretation; (3) Evidence-based medicine (EBM).
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · Decision-Making in Clinical Medicine

items.title: Understanding Clinical Expertise and Its Limitations
items.content: Clinical expertise in medicine is difficult to define and quantify, unlike other fields such as chess or athletics. Despite the presence of elite clinicians known for their problem-solving skills, these experts often cannot articulate their reasoning processes, limiting knowledge transfer. Additionally, clinical virtuosity is not generalizable across different medical conditions.
keyPoints: No objective ranking system exists for physicians post-training and board certification.
Elite clinicians' expertise may not extend to all areas of medicine.
Clinical expertise involves cognitive integration, fine-motor skills, communication, and care coordination.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · BRIEF INTRODUCTION TO CLINICAL REASONING (part 1)

items.title: Dual-Process Theory in Clinical Reasoning
items.content: The dual-process theory distinguishes between intuitive (System 1) and analytic (System 2) reasoning. Intuitive reasoning involves rapid, effortless judgments based on pattern recognition, while analytic reasoning is slow, methodical, and effortful. Experts often rely more on System 1, but both systems are part of a cognitive continuum and their interaction in decision-making is complex.
keyPoints: System 1 (Intuitive) involves rapid, effortless judgments using heuristics.
System 2 (Analytic) is slow, methodical, and effortful.
Experts use System 1 more effectively than novices but both systems are essential.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · BRIEF INTRODUCTION TO CLINICAL REASONING (part 1)

items.title: Pattern Recognition in Clinical Diagnosis
items.content: Experienced clinicians often recognize diagnostic patterns quickly through System 1 reasoning, which relies on a large library of stored patterns. In the absence of such familiarity, both students and experienced clinicians must use System 2 analytic reasoning for diagnosis. For example, hemoptysis in a healthy, nonsmoking man recovering from viral bronchitis is likely due to acute bronchitis.
keyPoints: Pattern recognition is rapid and often unconscious.
System 1 relies on stored diagnostic patterns.
In unfamiliar scenarios, System 2 analytic reasoning is necessary.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · BRIEF INTRODUCTION TO CLINICAL REASONING (part 1)

items.title: Recognizing Lung Carcinoma in Smokers
items.content: In patients with a significant smoking history, presenting with symptoms like productive cough with blood-streaked sputum and weight loss, consider lung carcinoma. Diagnostic steps include chest x-ray, sputum cytology, and referral for a CT scan.
keyPoints: 100-pack-year smoking history
Productive morning cough with blood-streaked sputum
Weight loss
Chest x-ray
Sputum cytology examination
Referral for chest computed tomography (CT) scan
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · BRIEF INTRODUCTION TO CLINICAL REASONING (part 2)

items.title: Evaluating Hemoptysis with Cardiac Auscultation Findings
items.content: In patients presenting with hemoptysis and a soft diastolic rumbling murmur at the apex, consider pulmonary hypertension from rheumatic mitral stenosis. An echocardiogram is warranted to assess for this condition.
keyPoints: 46-year-old patient
Hemoptysis
Soft diastolic rumbling murmur at the apex
Possible pulmonary hypertension
Suspected rheumatic mitral stenosis
Order echocardiogram
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · BRIEF INTRODUCTION TO CLINICAL REASONING (part 2)

items.title: Avoiding Premature Closure in Diagnosis
items.content: Ensure comprehensive evaluation to avoid premature closure. A case of a patient with 'flulike' symptoms misdiagnosed as acute bronchitis highlights the risk of relying solely on pattern recognition without thorough history and examination.
keyPoints: 45-year-old man
3-week history of 'flulike' upper respiratory infection (URI)
Dyspnea and productive cough
Absence of fever
Clear chest examination
Prescribed cough suppressant for acute bronchitis
Subsequent cardiac arrest due to myocardial infarction
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · BRIEF INTRODUCTION TO CLINICAL REASONING (part 2)

items.title: Understanding Heuristics in Clinical Diagnosis
items.content: Clinicians often rely on cognitive shortcuts, or heuristics, to diagnose patients. These include the representativeness heuristic, availability heuristic, anchoring heuristic, and simplicity heuristic. Each can lead to diagnostic errors if not carefully managed.
keyPoints: Representativeness heuristic: Overestimates disease likelihood based on symptom similarity without considering prevalence.
Availability heuristic: Influenced by memorable cases, leading to recall bias for rare outcomes.
Anchoring heuristic: Insufficient adjustment of initial disease probability after test results, contrary to Bayes' theorem.
Simplicity heuristic: Prefers simplest explanation but can lead to premature closure and neglect of significant symptoms.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · BRIEF INTRODUCTION TO CLINICAL REASONING (part 3)

items.title: Diagnostic Errors from Heuristics
items.content: Heuristics, while useful for quick decision-making, can lead to diagnostic errors. Clinicians must be aware of these pitfalls and adjust their reasoning accordingly.
keyPoints: Representativeness heuristic may cause overestimation of rare diseases with classic symptoms.
Availability heuristic biases judgment towards memorable but uncommon cases.
Anchoring heuristic leads to sticking with initial diagnoses despite new evidence.
Simplicity heuristic can result in overlooking complex or atypical presentations.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · BRIEF INTRODUCTION TO CLINICAL REASONING (part 3)

items.title: Analytic Reasoning for Complex Diagnoses
items.content: For complex or unfamiliar cases, clinicians should use analytic reasoning processes to systematically evaluate diagnostic possibilities.
keyPoints: Use the hypothetico-deductive model for methodical diagnosis refinement.
Develop and refine hypotheses based on patient history and physical examination.
Verify diagnoses by testing their adequacy and coherence with clinical findings.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · BRIEF INTRODUCTION TO CLINICAL REASONING (part 3)

items.title: Importance of Negative Findings in Reducing Diagnostic Likelihood
items.content: Negative findings play a crucial role in clinical reasoning by reducing the likelihood of certain diagnoses. For instance, chest discomfort not related to exertion or rest can lower suspicion for chronic ischemic heart disease.
keyPoints: Chest discomfort not provoked or worsened by exertion and not relieved by rest lowers likelihood of chronic ischemic heart disease.
Absence of resting tachycardia and thyroid gland enlargement reduces likelihood of hyperthyroidism in paroxysmal atrial fibrillation.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · BRIEF INTRODUCTION TO CLINICAL REASONING (part 4)

items.title: Diagnostic Imperatives for Acute Severe Chest Pain
items.content: In acute severe chest pain, clinicians must consider aortic dissection due to its potential fatality if untreated. Key actions include inquiring about suggestive symptoms, measuring blood pressures in both arms, and examining for pulse deficits.
keyPoints: Consider aortic dissection as a possible cause of acute severe chest discomfort.
Measure blood pressures in both arms for discrepancies and examine for pulse deficits.
If chest x-ray shows a possible widened mediastinum, reinstate hypothesis and order imaging (e.g., thoracic CT angiography or transesophageal echocardiogram).
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · BRIEF INTRODUCTION TO CLINICAL REASONING (part 4)

items.title: Role of Cognitive Shortcuts in Diagnostic Hypothesis Generation
items.content: Clinicians use cognitive shortcuts to manage the limited capacity of short-term memory, which can hold $7 ± 2$ items. This limitation necessitates the use of pattern matching and illness scripts for efficient diagnosis.
keyPoints: Short-term memory is limited to $7 ± 2$ items.
Cognitive shortcuts are essential for generating diagnostic hypotheses.
Experts use exemplars, prototypes, and illness scripts for pattern recognition.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · BRIEF INTRODUCTION TO CLINICAL REASONING (part 4)

items.subtopic: BRIEF INTRODUCTION TO CLINICAL REASONING (part 5) | PERSONALIZED DECISION-MAKING
items.title: The Role of Deliberate Practice in Developing Medical Expertise
items.content: While deliberate practice and personal coaching are strategies used to develop expertise in fields like music and athletics, their application in medicine remains underexplored. Studies suggest that exposure to disease pattern recognition and differential diagnosis is beneficial for medical education.
keyPoints: Deliberate effortful practice may take 10 years or 10,000 hours.
Personal coaching is used outside of medicine but not well-studied in it.
Exposure to signs/symptoms of diseases and differential diagnoses aids learning.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · BRIEF INTRODUCTION TO CLINICAL REASONING (part 5) | PERSONALIZED DECISION-MAKING

items.subtopic: BRIEF INTRODUCTION TO CLINICAL REASONING (part 5) | PERSONALIZED DECISION-MAKING
items.title: Personalized Decision-Making in Medicine
items.content: Personalizing treatment involves integrating evidence-based medicine with individual patient characteristics and preferences. This includes precision medicine for risk assessment and shared decision-making to align treatments with patient values.
keyPoints: Combine best available evidence with patient's unique features (risk factors, genomics, comorbidities).
Incorporate patient’s preferences and health goals in treatment decisions.
Shared decision-making involves clinicians and patients discussing options and outcomes.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · BRIEF INTRODUCTION TO CLINICAL REASONING (part 5) | PERSONALIZED DECISION-MAKING

items.subtopic: BRIEF INTRODUCTION TO CLINICAL REASONING (part 5) | PERSONALIZED DECISION-MAKING
items.title: Limitations of Individual Clinical Experience in Therapeutic Decision-Making
items.content: Relying on personal clinical experience for therapeutic decisions is unreliable due to nonrandom selection, small sample sizes, and rare events. Evidence from large patient groups is necessary to demonstrate treatment effectiveness.
keyPoints: Nonrandom selection and small sample sizes lead to erroneous causal inferences.
Treatment response is a counterfactual concept demonstrable statistically.
Therapeutic effectiveness should be based on evidence from clinical trials and outcome studies.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · BRIEF INTRODUCTION TO CLINICAL REASONING (part 5) | PERSONALIZED DECISION-MAKING

items.title: How Practice Style Influences Clinical Decisions
items.content: Physicians' practice styles, shaped by their training, experience, and beliefs about therapy effectiveness, significantly influence clinical decisions. For instance, cardiologists may prioritize detecting ischemia in low-risk chest pain patients using stress imaging, while internists might prefer exercise ECGs based on guideline recommendations.
keyPoints: Practice style defines norms of clinical behavior.
Specialists often know evidence better than generalists.
Cardiologists vs. internists: differing approaches to chest pain evaluation (stress imaging vs. exercise ECG).
The 'oculostenotic reflex' describes cardiologists' liberal use of coronary angiography and revascularization.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · NONCLINICAL INFLUENCES ON CLINICAL DECISION-MAKING

items.title: Impact of Defensive Medicine on Clinical Practice
items.content: Defensive medicine, driven by the fear of malpractice suits, leads physicians to order tests and therapies with minimal marginal benefits. This practice can become normalized, resulting in overuse of certain procedures, such as annual cardiac exercise testing in asymptomatic patients.
keyPoints: Defensive medicine is influenced by malpractice risk perceptions.
Tests and therapies are ordered with small marginal benefits to avoid criticism.
Over time, defensive practices may become accepted norms (e.g., annual cardiac tests in asymptomatic patients).
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · NONCLINICAL INFLUENCES ON CLINICAL DECISION-MAKING

items.title: Economic Incentives and Their Effect on Clinical Decisions
items.content: Payment systems, including fee-for-service, capitation, and salary models, influence clinical practice by creating economic incentives. Fee-for-service can encourage overuse of services, while capitation aims to reduce interventions with small marginal benefits. Current efforts focus on value-based payment systems to align payments with patient outcomes.
keyPoints: Fee-for-service encourages more services for higher pay.
Capitation provides fixed payments per patient to reduce unnecessary interventions.
Value-based payment systems aim to tie payments to clinical outcomes, though evidence is still lacking.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · NONCLINICAL INFLUENCES ON CLINICAL DECISION-MAKING

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSTIC TEST PERFORMANCE: UNDERSTANDING TEST ACCURACY
items.title: Importance of Gold Standard in Test Accuracy
items.content: The accuracy of a diagnostic test is best evaluated relative to a gold standard, which defines the presence or absence of disease. This comparison helps determine sensitivity and specificity.
keyPoints: Gold standard: Positive test confirms disease; negative test rules it out.
Sensitivity (true-positive rate): Proportion of patients with disease who test positive.
Specificity (true-negative rate): Proportion of patients without disease who test negative.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · DIAGNOSTIC TEST PERFORMANCE: UNDERSTANDING TEST ACCURACY

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSTIC TEST PERFORMANCE: UNDERSTANDING TEST ACCURACY
items.title: Sensitivity vs. Specificity Trade-off
items.content: Adjusting the threshold for a positive test affects sensitivity and specificity inversely. A higher cut point increases specificity but decreases sensitivity, while a lower cut point does the opposite.
keyPoints: High sensitivity (SnNout): Useful for ruling out disease when negative.
High specificity (SpPin): Useful for confirming disease when positive.
ROC curve: Graphically represents trade-off; area under curve indicates test information content.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · DIAGNOSTIC TEST PERFORMANCE: UNDERSTANDING TEST ACCURACY

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSTIC TEST PERFORMANCE: UNDERSTANDING TEST ACCURACY
items.title: Choosing the Appropriate Cut Point
items.content: The selection of a cut point for test positivity should consider treatment risks and benefits, as well as disease prevalence. This choice impacts the balance between false positives and negatives.
keyPoints: Cut point: Determines when a test result is positive or negative.
High-sensitivity cut points: Suitable for safe treatments with substantial benefit (e.g., phenylketonuria in newborns).
High-specificity cut points: Preferred when treatment has significant risks (e.g., chemotherapy for cancer).
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · DIAGNOSTIC TEST PERFORMANCE: UNDERSTANDING TEST ACCURACY

items.subtopic: MEASURES OF DISEASE PROBABILITY AND BAYES' RULE
items.title: Understanding Posttest Probability Using Bayes' Rule
items.content: Bayes' rule recalculates the probability of disease after obtaining a test result by integrating pretest probability, sensitivity, and specificity. It helps avoid anchoring bias in clinical decision-making.
keyPoints: Posttest probability is calculated using: Pretest probability x Test sensitivity / (Pretest probability x Test sensitivity + (1 - Pretest probability) x False-positive rate).
Example calculation for a 64-year-old woman with atypical chest pain, pretest probability of 0.50, test sensitivity and specificity both 0.90 results in posttest probability = 0.90.
Posttest probability is preferred over predictive value to avoid misinterpretation as intrinsic test accuracy.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · MEASURES OF DISEASE PROBABILITY AND BAYES' RULE

items.subtopic: MEASURES OF DISEASE PROBABILITY AND BAYES' RULE
items.title: Interpreting Likelihood Ratios in Diagnostic Testing
items.content: Likelihood ratios (LR) quantify how well a diagnostic test result distinguishes between patients with and without disease. They are derived from sensitivity and specificity.
keyPoints: Likelihood ratio for a positive test is calculated as Sensitivity / (1 - Specificity).
A likelihood ratio of 9 indicates that a positive result is 9 times more likely in those with the disease than without it.
Tests with high LR+ (>10) are useful for 'ruling in' disease, while low LR- (<0.10) values help 'rule out' disease.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · MEASURES OF DISEASE PROBABILITY AND BAYES' RULE

items.subtopic: MEASURES OF DISEASE PROBABILITY AND BAYES' RULE
items.title: Using Likelihood Ratios to Adjust Disease Probability
items.content: Likelihood ratios adjust the probability of a diagnosis by quantifying how much a test result changes the odds of having the disease.
keyPoints: A positive likelihood ratio (LR+) increases posttest probability, while a negative likelihood ratio (LR-) decreases it.
Example: A test with sensitivity 0.90 and specificity 0.90 has LR+ = 9 and LR- = 0.11.
High LR+ values (>10) suggest high specificity for 'ruling in' disease; low LR- values (<0.10) indicate high sensitivity for 'ruling out' disease.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · MEASURES OF DISEASE PROBABILITY AND BAYES' RULE

items.subtopic: APPLICATIONS TO DIAGNOSTIC TESTING IN CAD
items.title: Interpreting Posttest Probability Changes with Exercise Treadmill Test
items.content: The exercise treadmill test has modest discriminatory ability, reflected in its likelihood ratio positive of 2.4. This results in limited changes in posttest probability for CAD diagnosis, particularly when pretest probabilities are low or high.
keyPoints: Exercise treadmill ST-segment response sensitivity ~60%, specificity ~75%
Likelihood ratio positive = 2.4
Low pretest probability (10%) with a positive test yields posttest probability of ~30%
High pretest probability (80%) with a positive test yields posttest probability of ~95%
Limited impact on management decisions due to modest changes in diagnostic likelihood
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · APPLICATIONS TO DIAGNOSTIC TESTING IN CAD

items.subtopic: APPLICATIONS TO DIAGNOSTIC TESTING IN CAD
items.title: Impact of Exercise SPECT Myocardial Perfusion Test Accuracy
items.content: The exercise SPECT test, with higher sensitivity and specificity (both ~90%), provides better diagnostic accuracy for CAD. However, even this more accurate test may not significantly alter management decisions due to limited changes in posttest probabilities.
keyPoints: Exercise SPECT myocardial perfusion test sensitivity and specificity both ~90%
Likelihood ratio positive = 9.0
Low pretest probability (10%) with a positive test yields posttest probability of ~50%
High pretest probability (80%) with a positive test yields posttest probability of ~97%
Limited impact on management decisions despite higher accuracy
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · APPLICATIONS TO DIAGNOSTIC TESTING IN CAD

items.subtopic: APPLICATIONS TO DIAGNOSTIC TESTING IN CAD
items.title: Bayes' Rule and Test Result Interpretation in CAD Diagnosis
items.content: Bayes' rule simplifies test result interpretation but has limitations, such as assuming binary outcomes and conditional independence. Sensitivity and specificity may vary with disease prevalence and severity, affecting diagnostic accuracy.
keyPoints: Bayes' rule assumes binary test results; real tests often have multidimensional outcomes
Conditional independence assumption may not hold in sequential testing
Sensitivity varies by disease severity (e.g., ~30% for one-vessel CAD, ~80% for severe three-vessel CAD)
Prevalence affects sensitivity and specificity estimates
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · APPLICATIONS TO DIAGNOSTIC TESTING IN CAD

items.title: Why Use Multivariable Statistical Models for Risk Prediction?
items.content: Multivariable statistical models provide a more accurate prediction of complex clinical problems by considering multiple patient-specific factors simultaneously. These models assign weights to each factor based on its independent contribution, which can enhance the accuracy of predictions compared to simpler methods like Bayes' rule.
keyPoints: Models account for multiple overlapping pieces of patient-specific information.
Logistic regression models consider all relevant clinical examination and diagnostic testing factors.
Prediction models are complex and typically require computational tools.
Examples include the ACC/AHA risk calculator for statins and CHA2DS2-VASc for anticoagulation in atrial fibrillation.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · RISK PREDICTION MODELS

items.title: How Important is Independent Validation of Prediction Models?
items.content: Independent validation of prediction models in a separate population from the one used to develop them is crucial. Without sufficient validation, these models should be approached with skepticism similar to that for new drugs or medical devices lacking rigorous clinical trial testing.
keyPoints: Only a few models like Wells criteria for pulmonary embolism have been sufficiently validated.
Independent validation ensures the model's applicability and reliability in different populations.
Unvalidated models should be viewed with skepticism.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · RISK PREDICTION MODELS

items.subtopic: DECISION SUPPORT SYSTEMS AND ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
items.title: The Role of AI in Clinical Decision-Making
items.content: Artificial intelligence (AI) offers various levels of support for clinical decision-making, from providing access to information to simulating decision processes. However, its current utility in clinical care is limited despite advancements.
keyPoints: Computers can provide basic information access and higher-level decision management guidance using Bayesian or statistical regression models.
AI tools such as neural networks, machine learning (including deep learning), and generative AI are being explored for tasks like pattern recognition and content generation.
Large language models show promise in clinical note creation but require validation beyond their development populations.
Clinicians may rely on AI-based tools even when the information is inaccurate or contradictory.
Reminder systems, particularly those integrated into EHRs, have shown more promise by correcting drug dosing and promoting guideline adherence.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · DECISION SUPPORT SYSTEMS AND ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE

items.subtopic: DECISION SUPPORT SYSTEMS AND ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
items.title: Challenges and Considerations in Implementing AI Tools
items.content: While AI has potential, its implementation in clinical practice faces challenges such as model confabulation and the need for comprehensive testing to ensure patient safety.
keyPoints: AI-based tools are at a preliminary stage with limited demonstrated utility in clinical care.
Concerns about model confabulation necessitate careful testing before AI tools can be safely implemented.
Reminder or protocol-directed systems, unlike predictive models, use existing algorithms to guide practice and have shown more consistent benefits.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · DECISION SUPPORT SYSTEMS AND ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE

items.title: Optimal Breast Cancer Screening Strategy: Biennial vs. Annual
items.content: Decision analysis can guide complex health policy decisions, such as breast cancer screening strategies, by weighing benefits and risks in the absence of randomized trials.
keyPoints: Biennial screening for 1000 average-risk women aged 50–74 years avoids seven breast cancer deaths compared to no screening.
Annual screening from age 40–74 years prevents three additional deaths but requires 20,000 more mammograms and results in 1988 false positives.
For high-risk patients (two- to fourfold increased risk), annual screening from age 40–74 yields similar benefits as biennial screening from age 50–74.
Screening can be stopped earlier at age 66–68 for average-risk patients with moderate or severe comorbidities.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · DECISION ANALYSIS

items.title: Decision Analysis in Public Health Policy: Balancing Trade-offs
items.content: Decision analysis helps balance trade-offs in public health policy by considering factors like quality of life, resource use, and patient preferences.
keyPoints: Decision analysis is a normative approach used for complex decisions involving trade-offs such as length vs. quality of life and population vs. individual health.
The 2016 CISNET analysis evaluated eight breast cancer screening strategies using six simulation models.
Models accounted for epidemiologic trends, digital technology advances, treatments, quality of life, risk factors, breast density, and comorbidity.
While beneficial for public health policy recommendations, the application to individual clinical management remains unproven.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · DECISION ANALYSIS

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS AS AN ELEMENT OF QUALITY OF CARE | EVIDENCE-BASED MEDICINE
items.title: Diagnostic Errors as a Systemic Issue in Quality Care
items.content: The modern perspective on diagnostic errors emphasizes system-level deficiencies rather than individual clinician failures. Solutions focus on integrating decision support tools into electronic health records (EHRs) and using checklists to mitigate cognitive errors.
keyPoints: Diagnostic errors are a significant patient-safety issue, with nearly everyone expected to experience at least one in their lifetime.
System-level approaches like EHR integration and checklists aim to reduce diagnostic errors.
The effectiveness of checklists in preventing adverse events due to diagnostic errors is yet to be fully demonstrated.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · DIAGNOSIS AS AN ELEMENT OF QUALITY OF CARE | EVIDENCE-BASED MEDICINE

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS AS AN ELEMENT OF QUALITY OF CARE | EVIDENCE-BASED MEDICINE
items.title: Evidence-Based Medicine: A Structured Approach
items.content: EBM emphasizes a structured process for integrating scientific evidence into clinical practice, involving formulating questions, searching literature, appraising evidence, and applying it to patient care.
keyPoints: EBM involves four key steps: formulating management questions, searching research data, appraising evidence validity and relevance, and integrating findings with patient-specific knowledge.
Systematic reviews are considered high-level evidence but have limitations when few trials or mixed study types exist.
Clinicians often rely on systematic reviews as a shortcut due to the time-consuming nature of comprehensive literature appraisal.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · DIAGNOSIS AS AN ELEMENT OF QUALITY OF CARE | EVIDENCE-BASED MEDICINE

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS AS AN ELEMENT OF QUALITY OF CARE | EVIDENCE-BASED MEDICINE
items.title: Limitations and Use of Systematic Reviews in Clinical Practice
items.content: While systematic reviews are valuable for summarizing evidence, their utility is limited by the quality and quantity of underlying research. Clinicians should use them cautiously alongside empirical studies.
keyPoints: Systematic reviews summarize available evidence but cannot compensate for deficiencies in the original research.
The peer review process has not effectively ensured the quality of systematic reviews.
Clinicians should critically evaluate systematic reviews and supplement with high-quality empirical studies.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · DIAGNOSIS AS AN ELEMENT OF QUALITY OF CARE | EVIDENCE-BASED MEDICINE

items.subtopic: SOURCES OF EVIDENCE: CLINICAL TRIALS AND REGISTRIES (part 1)
items.title: Role and Limitations of Observational Studies in Evidence Generation
items.content: Observational studies, while valuable for generating hypotheses and providing initial reports on adverse events or therapeutic benefits, have limitations in validity and generalizability. They cannot replace randomized clinical trials in establishing modern standards of practice due to their susceptibility to treatment selection bias.
keyPoints: Observational studies can generate important hypotheses but lack the rigor needed for formulating modern practice standards.
They often suffer from substantial limitations in validity and generalizability.
Treatment selection bias is a major limitation, as observational data do not control patient care.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · SOURCES OF EVIDENCE: CLINICAL TRIALS AND REGISTRIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: SOURCES OF EVIDENCE: CLINICAL TRIALS AND REGISTRIES (part 1)
items.title: Importance of Randomized Controlled Trials
items.content: Randomized controlled trials are the gold standard for developing reliable evidence due to their design, which includes random allocation of treatment. This feature provides strong protection against confounding from treatment selection bias, ensuring high internal validity.
keyPoints: Randomized clinical trials offer the best protection against measured and unmeasured confounding.
They include careful prospective design features similar to observational studies but with added randomization for treatment allocation.
While they have high internal validity, their external validity may be limited by recruitment processes or heterogeneous trial populations.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · SOURCES OF EVIDENCE: CLINICAL TRIALS AND REGISTRIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: SOURCES OF EVIDENCE: CLINICAL TRIALS AND REGISTRIES (part 1)
items.title: Advantages of Observational Data in Clinical Research
items.content: Observational data can include a broader population than clinical trials, providing primary evidence for research questions where randomized trials are impractical or unethical. They allow for the study of unproven but widely accepted practices and conditions that cannot be ethically randomized.
keyPoints: Observational studies encompass a broader patient population than clinical trials due to less restrictive inclusion criteria.
They provide essential evidence when randomized trials are not feasible, such as with certain diagnostic or therapeutic strategies.
Ethical considerations preclude randomization in some scenarios, making observational data crucial for research.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · SOURCES OF EVIDENCE: CLINICAL TRIALS AND REGISTRIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: SOURCES OF EVIDENCE: CLINICAL TRIALS AND REGISTRIES (part 2) | META-ANALYSIS
items.title: Understanding the Limitations of Randomized Trials for Clinical Practice
items.content: Randomized trials, while crucial for FDA approval and evidence generation, often involve design compromises that limit their direct applicability to clinical practice. These trials may use placebo controls or select populations that do not reflect real-world settings.
keyPoints: Trials designed for FDA approval may have different populations and designs than those useful in practice.
Placebo control is a common requirement that can affect trial relevance.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · SOURCES OF EVIDENCE: CLINICAL TRIALS AND REGISTRIES (part 2) | META-ANALYSIS

items.subtopic: SOURCES OF EVIDENCE: CLINICAL TRIALS AND REGISTRIES (part 2) | META-ANALYSIS
items.title: The Role and Reliability of Meta-Analyses in Evidence Synthesis
items.content: Meta-analysis is a quantitative method to summarize evidence from multiple studies, ideally including unpublished trials to avoid bias. It is most effective when combining several large-scale, well-conducted randomized trials.
keyPoints: Meta-analyses should include individual patient-level data for reliability.
They are persuasive with several large-scale, properly performed trials.
Publication bias can be mitigated by including unpublished trials.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · SOURCES OF EVIDENCE: CLINICAL TRIALS AND REGISTRIES (part 2) | META-ANALYSIS

items.subtopic: SOURCES OF EVIDENCE: CLINICAL TRIALS AND REGISTRIES (part 2) | META-ANALYSIS
items.title: Interpreting Number Needed to Treat (NNT) in Clinical Contexts
items.content: NNT is a measure of treatment efficiency, calculated as the inverse of absolute risk reduction. It varies with patient risk levels and should be interpreted with caution due to assumptions about treatment response homogeneity.
keyPoints: NNT = 1/ARR; e.g., if ARR is 4%, NNT is 25.
NNT changes with the baseline risk of the population (e.g., higher risk, lower NNT).
Assumes homogeneous treatment response, which may not be accurate.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · SOURCES OF EVIDENCE: CLINICAL TRIALS AND REGISTRIES (part 2) | META-ANALYSIS

items.title: Role and Limitations of Clinical Practice Guidelines in Decision-Making
items.content: Clinical practice guidelines are systematically developed statements designed to assist practitioners and patients with decisions about appropriate healthcare for specific clinical circumstances. They utilize evidence-based medicine (EBM) tools, focusing on disorders or interventions like diabetes mellitus or cancer screening. While they aim to improve care quality by recommending practices supported by high-quality evidence and favorable benefit-to-harm ratios, guidelines are meant to assist rather than dictate decisions in individual cases.
keyPoints: Guidelines use EBM tools including systematic literature searches and peer-reviewed literature reviews.
Focused on specific clinical disorders or healthcare interventions.
Aim to improve medical care quality by recommending evidence-supported practices with high benefit-to-harm ratios.
Intended to assist decision-making, not prescribe decisions for individual cases.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · CLINICAL PRACTICE GUIDELINES

items.title: Development and Composition of Clinical Practice Guidelines
items.content: Clinical practice guidelines are narrative documents created by expert panels often organized by professional bodies. These panels vary in expertise and stakeholder representation, impacting the guideline's recommendations. Each document includes specific management recommendations, evidence quality assessments, benefit-to-harm ratios, and a narrative discussion to guide clinical practice.
keyPoints: Guidelines are narrative documents developed by expert panels.
Panels' composition varies in expertise and stakeholder representation.
Documents include management recommendations, evidence quality, benefit-to-harm ratio assessments, and narrative discussions.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · CLINICAL PRACTICE GUIDELINES

items.question: According to the passage, what does Sir William Osler's quote about medicine emphasize?
options: Medicine is a science of certainty and an art of predictability.
Medicine is a science of uncertainty and an art of probability.
Medicine requires only technical knowledge for mastery.
Medicine operates with Newtonian predictability.
items.explanation: The quote emphasizes the dual nature of medicine as both uncertain in its scientific aspects and probabilistic in its practice.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · Decision-Making in Clinical Medicine

items.question: What is one reason why mastering technical knowledge alone does not ensure mastery of medical practice?
options: Technical knowledge is irrelevant to clinical practice.
Medical practice involves an element of irreducible uncertainty.
Modern medicine has become simpler over time.
Clinical reasoning is unnecessary for diagnosis.
items.explanation: The passage states that every aspect of medical practice includes irreducible uncertainty, which must be acknowledged by clinicians.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · Decision-Making in Clinical Medicine

items.question: What is one of the three pillars upon which modern medicine rests according to the passage?
options: Expertise in surgical techniques.
Rational diagnostic test use and interpretation.
Exclusive reliance on clinical judgment.
Avoidance of evidence-based practices.
items.explanation: The passage identifies rational diagnostic test use and interpretation as one of the pillars of modern medicine.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · Decision-Making in Clinical Medicine

items.question: Which of the following best describes why clinical expertise in medicine is difficult to define?
options: There are no objective ranking systems similar to those used in chess or athletics.
Clinical expertise can be easily generalized across different medical conditions.
All clinicians have an extensive library of stored diagnostic patterns.
Medical education provides a single integrated model of clinical reasoning.
items.explanation: Unlike chess and athletics, medicine lacks objective ranking systems to identify the highest levels of clinical performance.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · BRIEF INTRODUCTION TO CLINICAL REASONING (part 1)

items.question: What is a limitation in acquiring and disseminating clinical expertise according to the passage?
options: Elite clinicians can easily explain their methods.
Clinical virtuosity is generalizable across different medical conditions.
Master clinicians often cannot articulate their exact processes and methods.
Research on medical expertise is abundant and comprehensive.
items.explanation: Even master clinicians typically cannot explain their exact processes, limiting the acquisition and dissemination of their expertise.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · BRIEF INTRODUCTION TO CLINICAL REASONING (part 1)

items.question: According to the passage, what is a key component for rapid pattern recognition in clinical reasoning?
options: Extensive use of System 2 analytic approach.
A large library of stored diagnostic patterns.
Generalized medical knowledge applicable to all conditions.
Frequent errors in judgment during decision-making.
items.explanation: Experienced clinicians recognize familiar diagnostic patterns quickly due to a large library of stored patterns that can be rapidly accessed.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · BRIEF INTRODUCTION TO CLINICAL REASONING (part 1)

items.question: What does the dual-process theory in clinical reasoning distinguish between?
options: The importance and irrelevance of medical education.
Fast, effortless judgments and slow, methodical deliberations.
Generalized expertise across all medical conditions.
The role of intuition versus empirical studies.
items.explanation: Dual-process theory distinguishes between fast or intuitive reasoning (System 1) and slow, analytic reasoning (System 2).
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · BRIEF INTRODUCTION TO CLINICAL REASONING (part 1)

items.question: In the second scenario described, what additional tests did the clinician order for a patient with a history of smoking and symptoms suggestive of lung carcinoma?
options: Sputum cytology examination and chest CT scan
Echocardiogram and ECG
Pulmonary function test and bronchoscopy
MRI of the chest and PET scan
items.explanation: The clinician ordered a sputum cytology examination and referred for a chest CT scan.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · BRIEF INTRODUCTION TO CLINICAL REASONING (part 2)

items.question: What was the critical error made by the ED clinician in the case of the 45-year-old man with dyspnea and productive cough?
options: Misdiagnosed acute bronchitis
Failed to conduct a full history taking
Prescribed incorrect medication
Did not perform an echocardiogram
items.explanation: The clinician failed to elicit the full dyspnea history, which was crucial for diagnosing myocardial infarction.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · BRIEF INTRODUCTION TO CLINICAL REASONING (part 2)

items.question: What does 'premature closure' refer to in clinical reasoning?
options: Completing a patient's history before examination
Jumping to a diagnosis without all relevant data
Closing the medical record prematurely
Ending a consultation early
items.explanation: 'Premature closure' refers to mistakenly concluding on a diagnosis before having all necessary information.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · BRIEF INTRODUCTION TO CLINICAL REASONING (part 2)

items.question: Which research program suggests that simple heuristics can produce good decisions?
options: Heuristics and biases
Fast and frugal heuristics
Systematic reflection
Diagnostic verification
items.explanation: The 'fast and frugal heuristics' program explores the effectiveness of simple heuristics in decision-making.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · BRIEF INTRODUCTION TO CLINICAL REASONING (part 2)

items.question: Which cognitive shortcut involves clinicians overestimating a disease's likelihood based on the presence of representative symptoms, without considering its low underlying prevalence?
options: Representativeness heuristic
Availability heuristic
Anchoring heuristic
Simplicity heuristic
items.explanation: The representativeness heuristic leads clinicians to overestimate disease likelihood based on symptom similarity, ignoring low prevalence.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · BRIEF INTRODUCTION TO CLINICAL REASONING (part 3)

items.question: What cognitive shortcut involves judgments based on the ease of recalling similar cases or outcomes?
options: Representativeness heuristic
Availability heuristic
Anchoring heuristic
Simplicity heuristic
items.explanation: The availability heuristic is based on how easily prior similar cases can be recalled.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · BRIEF INTRODUCTION TO CLINICAL REASONING (part 3)

items.question: Which heuristic involves insufficiently adjusting the initial probability of disease after a test result, contrary to Bayes' theorem?
options: Representativeness heuristic
Availability heuristic
Anchoring heuristic
Simplicity heuristic
items.explanation: The anchoring heuristic involves sticking to an initial diagnosis despite new test results.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · BRIEF INTRODUCTION TO CLINICAL REASONING (part 3)

items.question: What principle suggests using the simplest explanation that accounts for a patient's symptoms?
options: Representativeness heuristic
Availability heuristic
Anchoring heuristic
Simplicity heuristic
items.explanation: The simplicity heuristic, or Occam's razor, suggests using the simplest explanation for symptoms.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · BRIEF INTRODUCTION TO CLINICAL REASONING (part 3)

items.question: Which of the following is an example of a 'diagnostic imperative' in clinical reasoning?
options: Considering hyperthyroidism when a patient has paroxysmal atrial fibrillation.
Routinely considering aortic dissection as a possible cause of acute severe chest discomfort.
Using prevalence to guide the generation of diagnostic hypotheses in nonacute situations.
Grouping data into packets or 'chunks' for storage in short-term memory.
items.explanation: Aortic dissection is considered a diagnostic imperative due to its potential fatality if undiagnosed and mistreated, despite being less prevalent than other conditions like MI.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · BRIEF INTRODUCTION TO CLINICAL REASONING (part 4)

items.question: What does the absence of a widened mediastinum on chest x-ray suggest in the context of acute severe chest pain?
options: The need to consider other causes like myocardial infarction.
That the hypothesis of aortic dissection should be discarded.
That blood pressure discrepancies between arms are likely present.
An immediate requirement for thoracic CT angiography.
items.explanation: If chest x-ray shows no widened mediastinum, clinicians may feel reassured to discard the aortic dissection hypothesis.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · BRIEF INTRODUCTION TO CLINICAL REASONING (part 4)

items.question: What is the role of cognitive shortcuts in clinical reasoning?
options: They allow clinicians to memorize large amounts of medical information.
They help generate and discard diagnostic hypotheses rapidly due to limited short-term memory.
They ensure that all potential diagnoses are considered equally.
They replace the need for pattern matching in diagnosis.
items.explanation: Cognitive shortcuts play a key role because they allow clinicians to manage the limited capacity of short-term memory by rapidly generating and discarding diagnostic hypotheses.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · BRIEF INTRODUCTION TO CLINICAL REASONING (part 4)

items.question: What is an essential component of expertise in medical diagnosis according to the passage?
options: Having a photographic memory of medical textbooks.
The ability to make powerful distinctions between diseases based on specific features.
Relying solely on pattern matching for diagnosing new cases.
Memorizing all possible symptoms and signs of every disease.
items.explanation: Expertise involves the ability to make powerful distinctions by connecting symptoms, signs, and risk factors meaningfully to differentiate between diseases.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · BRIEF INTRODUCTION TO CLINICAL REASONING (part 4)

items.subtopic: BRIEF INTRODUCTION TO CLINICAL REASONING (part 5) | PERSONALIZED DECISION-MAKING
items.question: Which of the following strategies are often used outside medicine to develop expertise?
options: Deliberate effortful practice and personal coaching
Rote memorization and passive observation
Group discussions and theoretical lectures
Self-assessment quizzes and peer reviews
items.explanation: Deliberate effortful practice and personal coaching are strategies used outside medicine to develop expertise.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · BRIEF INTRODUCTION TO CLINICAL REASONING (part 5) | PERSONALIZED DECISION-MAKING

items.subtopic: BRIEF INTRODUCTION TO CLINICAL REASONING (part 5) | PERSONALIZED DECISION-MAKING
items.question: What is a key component of personalized therapeutic decision-making in modern medical practice?
options: Relying solely on physician impressions from personal experience
Combining evidence with patient's unique features and preferences
Using only large-scale clinical trials for every individual case
Focusing exclusively on disease pattern recognition
items.explanation: Personalized therapeutic decision-making involves combining the best available evidence with an individual patient’s unique features and their preferences.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · BRIEF INTRODUCTION TO CLINICAL REASONING (part 5) | PERSONALIZED DECISION-MAKING

items.subtopic: BRIEF INTRODUCTION TO CLINICAL REASONING (part 5) | PERSONALIZED DECISION-MAKING
items.question: Why is it incorrect to infer that treating a hypertensive patient with ACE inhibitors necessarily prevented a stroke?
options: Because treatment response for chronic diseases can only be demonstrated statistically in large populations
Because all patients respond positively to ACE inhibitors
Due to the immediate observable effects of blood pressure lowering
Since ACE inhibitors are not effective for hypertension
items.explanation: Treatment response is a counterfactual concept, only demonstrable statistically in large patient populations.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · BRIEF INTRODUCTION TO CLINICAL REASONING (part 5) | PERSONALIZED DECISION-MAKING

items.subtopic: BRIEF INTRODUCTION TO CLINICAL REASONING (part 5) | PERSONALIZED DECISION-MAKING
items.question: What should therapeutic decision-making be based on according to the passage?
options: Physician impressions of benefit and harm from personal experience
Observations of individual patient outcomes
The best available evidence from clinical trials and outcome studies
General guidelines without considering individual patient differences
items.explanation: Therapeutic decision-making should be based on the best available evidence from clinical trials and well-done outcome studies.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · BRIEF INTRODUCTION TO CLINICAL REASONING (part 5) | PERSONALIZED DECISION-MAKING

items.question: Which factor primarily influences a physician's practice style?
options: The availability of advanced imaging facilities.
Physician knowledge, training, and experience.
Local malpractice suit statistics.
Fee-for-service payment systems.
items.explanation: Practice style is influenced by a physician's knowledge, training, and experience.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · NONCLINICAL INFLUENCES ON CLINICAL DECISION-MAKING

items.question: What does the term 'defensive medicine' refer to?
options: A practice style focused on maximizing patient outcomes.
Ordering tests and therapies with small marginal benefits to avoid malpractice suits.
The use of electronic health records to improve workflow efficiency.
Adopting value-based payment systems.
items.explanation: Defensive medicine involves ordering tests and therapies to preclude criticism from potential adverse outcomes.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · NONCLINICAL INFLUENCES ON CLINICAL DECISION-MAKING

items.question: How does the fee-for-service payment system potentially influence clinical practice?
options: It encourages underuse of medical services.
It promotes a fixed payment per patient to reduce interventions.
It incentivizes physicians to perform more procedures for higher payments.
It discourages the use of advanced imaging technologies.
items.explanation: Fee-for-service encourages overuse as physicians get paid more when they do more.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · NONCLINICAL INFLUENCES ON CLINICAL DECISION-MAKING

items.question: What is a key goal of transitioning to value-based payment systems?
options: To increase the number of services provided.
To tie payments to the quality and benefit of care.
To ensure all physicians receive equal compensation.
To eliminate the need for clinical trials.
items.explanation: Value-based payment systems aim to reduce overuse by tying payments to the value and benefit of care provided.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · NONCLINICAL INFLUENCES ON CLINICAL DECISION-MAKING

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSTIC TEST PERFORMANCE: UNDERSTANDING TEST ACCURACY
items.question: What does a sensitivity of 100% indicate about a diagnostic test?
options: The test correctly identifies all patients without the disease.
The test correctly identifies all patients with the disease.
The test has no false-positive results.
The test has no false-negative results.
items.explanation: Sensitivity reflects how well a test identifies patients with disease. A sensitivity of 100% means it correctly identifies all patients with the disease.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · DIAGNOSTIC TEST PERFORMANCE: UNDERSTANDING TEST ACCURACY

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSTIC TEST PERFORMANCE: UNDERSTANDING TEST ACCURACY
items.question: Which statement best describes specificity in diagnostic testing?
options: It is the proportion of patients with the disease who have a positive test.
It is the proportion of patients without the disease who have a negative test.
It is calculated as 1 minus sensitivity.
It indicates how well a test identifies patients with disease.
items.explanation: Specificity reflects how well a test correctly identifies patients without the disease, which is the proportion of patients without the disease who have a negative test.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · DIAGNOSTIC TEST PERFORMANCE: UNDERSTANDING TEST ACCURACY

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSTIC TEST PERFORMANCE: UNDERSTANDING TEST ACCURACY
items.question: What happens to sensitivity when the cut point for a positive test result is made stricter?
options: Sensitivity increases.
Sensitivity decreases.
Specificity decreases.
There is no change in sensitivity.
items.explanation: Making the cut point stricter lowers sensitivity because fewer patients with disease will meet the higher threshold for a positive test result.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · DIAGNOSTIC TEST PERFORMANCE: UNDERSTANDING TEST ACCURACY

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSTIC TEST PERFORMANCE: UNDERSTANDING TEST ACCURACY
items.question: What does an area under the ROC curve of 0.5 indicate?
options: The test is perfect.
The test has no diagnostic information.
The test's sensitivity and specificity are both 100%.
The test's false-positive rate equals its true-negative rate.
items.explanation: An area under the ROC curve of 0.5 indicates that the test provides no diagnostic information, equivalent to flipping a coin.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · DIAGNOSTIC TEST PERFORMANCE: UNDERSTANDING TEST ACCURACY

items.subtopic: MEASURES OF DISEASE PROBABILITY AND BAYES' RULE
items.question: Which of the following parameters is NOT used to calculate posttest probability using Bayes' rule?
options: Pretest probability
Test sensitivity
Test specificity
Patient's age
items.explanation: Posttest probability is calculated using pretest probability, test sensitivity, and test specificity.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · MEASURES OF DISEASE PROBABILITY AND BAYES' RULE

items.subtopic: MEASURES OF DISEASE PROBABILITY AND BAYES' RULE
items.question: What does the likelihood ratio for a positive test indicate?
options: The ratio of true-negative rate to false-positive rate
The probability of disease in patients without it
How well the test distinguishes those with from those without disease
The pretest probability
items.explanation: The likelihood ratio for a positive test is the ratio of the probability of a given test result in a patient with disease to that in a patient without disease.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · MEASURES OF DISEASE PROBABILITY AND BAYES' RULE

items.subtopic: MEASURES OF DISEASE PROBABILITY AND BAYES' RULE
items.question: What mnemonic helps remember that a high-specificity positive test 'rules in' disease?
options: SnNout
SpPin
PPV
NPV
items.explanation: 'SpPin' is the mnemonic for remembering that a positive result from a high-specificity test helps rule in disease.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · MEASURES OF DISEASE PROBABILITY AND BAYES' RULE

items.subtopic: MEASURES OF DISEASE PROBABILITY AND BAYES' RULE
items.question: What does a likelihood ratio negative value below 0.10 usually imply?
options: Low sensitivity
High specificity
High pretest probability
Low test accuracy
items.explanation: A very low likelihood ratio negative (below 0.10) usually implies high sensitivity, helping to rule out disease.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · MEASURES OF DISEASE PROBABILITY AND BAYES' RULE

items.subtopic: APPLICATIONS TO DIAGNOSTIC TESTING IN CAD
items.question: What is the likelihood ratio positive for a positive exercise treadmill ST-segment response?
items.explanation: The likelihood ratio positive for a positive exercise treadmill ST-segment response is calculated as sensitivity divided by (1 - specificity), which equals 2.4.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · APPLICATIONS TO DIAGNOSTIC TESTING IN CAD

items.subtopic: APPLICATIONS TO DIAGNOSTIC TESTING IN CAD
items.question: What posttest probability of CAD does a positive exercise SPECT test yield for a patient with a pretest probability of 10%?
items.explanation: A positive exercise SPECT test raises the posttest probability to 50% for a patient with an initial 10% pretest probability.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · APPLICATIONS TO DIAGNOSTIC TESTING IN CAD

items.subtopic: APPLICATIONS TO DIAGNOSTIC TESTING IN CAD
items.question: For which pretest probability range does the largest change in diagnostic likelihood occur after testing?
items.explanation: The largest change in diagnostic likelihood following a test result occurs when the pretest probability is between 30 and 70%.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · APPLICATIONS TO DIAGNOSTIC TESTING IN CAD

items.subtopic: APPLICATIONS TO DIAGNOSTIC TESTING IN CAD
items.question: What posttest probability of CAD does a positive exercise SPECT perfusion test yield for a patient with a pretest probability of 50%?
items.explanation: A positive exercise SPECT perfusion test raises the posttest probability to 90% for a patient with an initial pretest probability of 50%.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · APPLICATIONS TO DIAGNOSTIC TESTING IN CAD

items.question: Which of the following is a reason why prediction models are not widely used in clinical practice?
options: They require computational resources such as calculators or computers.
They always provide more accurate predictions than clinicians.
They are embedded into electronic health record systems.
They have been validated sufficiently for all conditions.
items.explanation: Prediction models are often too complex computationally to use without a calculator or computer, limiting their clinical use.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · RISK PREDICTION MODELS

items.question: What is essential for the validation of risk prediction models?
options: Validation in the same population used for development.
Independent validation in a separate population.
Embedding into electronic health record systems.
Use by clinicians without additional training.
items.explanation: The importance of independent validation in a population separate from the one used to develop the model is crucial for risk prediction models.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · RISK PREDICTION MODELS

items.question: What has been observed about the impact of EHR-based predictive models on patient outcomes?
options: They have consistently improved patient outcomes.
The evidence is mostly observational and suggests more work is needed.
They are always accurate in predicting clinical outcomes.
They eliminate the need for clinician judgment.
items.explanation: Evidence about the impact of EHR-based models on patient outcomes is mostly observational, indicating that further research is necessary.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · RISK PREDICTION MODELS

items.subtopic: DECISION SUPPORT SYSTEMS AND ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
items.question: Which of the following is NOT a subtype of artificial intelligence mentioned in the passage?
options: Neural networks
Machine learning
Deep reinforcement learning
Generative AI
items.explanation: The passage mentions neural networks, machine learning (including deep learning), and generative AI as subtypes of artificial intelligence.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · DECISION SUPPORT SYSTEMS AND ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE

items.subtopic: DECISION SUPPORT SYSTEMS AND ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
items.question: What is the primary purpose of reminder or protocol-directed systems in clinical practice?
options: Making predictions
Generating new content
Directing clinical practice using existing algorithms
Providing algorithmic guidance
items.explanation: Reminder or protocol-directed systems use existing algorithms, such as guidelines or appropriate utilization criteria, to direct clinical practice.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · DECISION SUPPORT SYSTEMS AND ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE

items.subtopic: DECISION SUPPORT SYSTEMS AND ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
items.question: What is a concern associated with the implementation of AI tools in clinical care?
options: Lack of enthusiasm among clinicians
Inability to generate language-based content
Potential for patient harms due to model confabulation
Difficulty accessing information
items.explanation: Concerns about model confabulation and the potential for patient harms mandate careful and comprehensive testing before AI tools are implemented in clinical care.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · DECISION SUPPORT SYSTEMS AND ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE

items.question: Which screening strategy was found to be the most efficient for average-risk women according to the CISNET analysis?
options: Annual screening from age 40–74 years
Biennial screening from age 50–74 years
Hybrid screening starting annually at age 40 and biennially thereafter
No screening
items.explanation: The six simulation models found biennial strategies to be the most efficient for average-risk women.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · DECISION ANALYSIS

items.question: What was a major challenge in determining the optimal screening strategy for breast cancer?
options: Lack of digital technology advancements
Inability to conduct randomized controlled trials due to impracticality
Absence of epidemiologic trends data
Insufficient quality of life considerations
items.explanation: A randomized controlled trial to determine when to begin screening and how often to repeat screening mammography is impractical.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · DECISION ANALYSIS

items.question: What additional burden did annual screening from age 40–74 years impose compared to biennial screening from age 50–74?
options: Fewer breast cancer deaths avoided
20,000 fewer mammograms required
1988 fewer false-positive results
Increased number of mammograms and more false-positive results
items.explanation: Annual screening from age 40–74 years avoided three additional deaths but required 20,000 additional mammograms and yielded 1988 more false-positive results.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · DECISION ANALYSIS

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS AS AN ELEMENT OF QUALITY OF CARE | EVIDENCE-BASED MEDICINE
items.question: Which of the following is NOT a method used to estimate the incidence of diagnostic errors?
options: Postmortem examinations
Medical record reviews
Patient surveys
Medical malpractice claims
items.explanation: Patient surveys are not mentioned as a method for estimating diagnostic error incidence.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · DIAGNOSIS AS AN ELEMENT OF QUALITY OF CARE | EVIDENCE-BASED MEDICINE

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS AS AN ELEMENT OF QUALITY OF CARE | EVIDENCE-BASED MEDICINE
items.question: What is the modern view of the cause of most diagnostic errors?
options: Failures of individual clinicians
System deficiencies in care
Lack of medical knowledge
Inadequate patient communication
items.explanation: The modern view is that diagnostic errors are mostly due to system deficiencies in care.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · DIAGNOSIS AS AN ELEMENT OF QUALITY OF CARE | EVIDENCE-BASED MEDICINE

items.question: What is the primary purpose of systematic reviews in evidence-based medicine?
options: To provide a narrative overview of research
To summarize available evidence comprehensively
To replace individual clinical reports
To conduct new experimental studies
items.explanation: Systematic reviews aim to comprehensively summarize the available evidence on a particular topic.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · DIAGNOSIS AS AN ELEMENT OF QUALITY OF CARE | EVIDENCE-BASED MEDICINE

items.question: Why might systematic reviews be less valuable in certain circumstances?
options: When there are many trials available
When only observational studies exist
When they are peer-reviewed
When they include meta-analyses
items.explanation: Systematic reviews may be less valuable when only a few trials or mixed study types are available, or the evidence base is primarily observational.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · DIAGNOSIS AS AN ELEMENT OF QUALITY OF CARE | EVIDENCE-BASED MEDICINE

items.subtopic: SOURCES OF EVIDENCE: CLINICAL TRIALS AND REGISTRIES (part 1)
items.question: Which of the following best explains why early COVID-19 publications were often contradictory?
options: They primarily consisted of large, high-quality observational registries.
Many were small observational series or reviews with limited validity and generalizability.
The majority were randomized clinical trials with robust design features.
Most studies focused on well-defined eligibility criteria and interventions.
items.explanation: Early COVID-19 publications often consisted of small observational series or reviews, which have substantial limitations in validity and generalizability.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · SOURCES OF EVIDENCE: CLINICAL TRIALS AND REGISTRIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: SOURCES OF EVIDENCE: CLINICAL TRIALS AND REGISTRIES (part 1)
items.question: What is a major advantage of using observational data over randomized clinical trials?
options: Observational data are always more reliable than randomized trials.
They include a broader population as encountered in practice.
They eliminate treatment selection bias completely.
Observational studies have better external validity.
items.explanation: Observational data include a broader population, unlike clinical trials which often have restrictive inclusion and exclusion criteria.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · SOURCES OF EVIDENCE: CLINICAL TRIALS AND REGISTRIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: SOURCES OF EVIDENCE: CLINICAL TRIALS AND REGISTRIES (part 1)
items.question: Why might observational studies not provide reliable evidence in certain cases?
options: They always include random allocation of treatment.
Clinical management is completely random, ensuring no bias.
Management may be too confounded for statistical correction.
Observational data are collected retrospectively.
items.explanation: When clinical management is not random and the problem is too confounded, observational studies may not provide reliable evidence.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · SOURCES OF EVIDENCE: CLINICAL TRIALS AND REGISTRIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: SOURCES OF EVIDENCE: CLINICAL TRIALS AND REGISTRIES (part 1)
items.question: Which type of control is generally preferable in clinical research?
options: Historical controls
Concurrent controls
Retrospective controls
Prospective controls
items.explanation: The use of concurrent controls is vastly preferable to that of historical controls.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · SOURCES OF EVIDENCE: CLINICAL TRIALS AND REGISTRIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: SOURCES OF EVIDENCE: CLINICAL TRIALS AND REGISTRIES (part 2) | META-ANALYSIS
items.question: Which of the following is NOT a reason why meta-analysis can be particularly useful?
options: It combines and summarizes available evidence quantitatively.
It helps detect benefits when individual trials are inadequately powered.
It ensures all unpublished trials are included to avoid publication bias.
It provides unique information beyond absolute treatment differences.
items.explanation: Meta-analysis does not provide unique information beyond summarizing absolute treatment differences.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · SOURCES OF EVIDENCE: CLINICAL TRIALS AND REGISTRIES (part 2) | META-ANALYSIS

items.subtopic: SOURCES OF EVIDENCE: CLINICAL TRIALS AND REGISTRIES (part 2) | META-ANALYSIS
items.question: What is a critical factor to consider when interpreting the results of a meta-analysis?
options: The number of trials included in the analysis.
Whether individual patient-level data were analyzed.
The homogeneity in response to treatment assumed by NNT.
All of the above.
items.explanation: All listed factors are critical when interpreting meta-analysis results, including trial numbers, data level, and assumptions about treatment response.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · SOURCES OF EVIDENCE: CLINICAL TRIALS AND REGISTRIES (part 2) | META-ANALYSIS

items.subtopic: SOURCES OF EVIDENCE: CLINICAL TRIALS AND REGISTRIES (part 2) | META-ANALYSIS
items.question: What is the primary metric used in meta-analyses to summarize relative treatment benefit?
options: Absolute risk reduction.
Number needed to treat.
Odds ratios or relative risks.
Patient-level data.
items.explanation: Meta-analyses typically focus on summary measures like odds ratios or relative risks for relative treatment benefit.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · SOURCES OF EVIDENCE: CLINICAL TRIALS AND REGISTRIES (part 2) | META-ANALYSIS

items.subtopic: SOURCES OF EVIDENCE: CLINICAL TRIALS AND REGISTRIES (part 2) | META-ANALYSIS
items.question: Why should clinicians also examine absolute risk reduction (ARR) in addition to relative treatment benefits?
options: Because ARR provides unique information not available from relative measures.
To understand the actual impact of therapy on patient outcomes.
Because meta-analyses are unreliable without considering ARR.
To ensure that all trials included were adequately powered.
items.explanation: Clinicians should examine ARR to understand the real-world impact of a therapy, beyond just relative measures.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · SOURCES OF EVIDENCE: CLINICAL TRIALS AND REGISTRIES (part 2) | META-ANALYSIS

items.question: What is the primary objective of clinical practice guidelines?
options: To define explicitly what decisions should be made in a particular situation.
To improve the quality of medical care by identifying routinely implementable practices based on high-quality evidence and benefit-to-harm ratios.
To replace the need for systematic literature searches in guideline development.
To serve as the sole basis for clinical decision-making.
items.explanation: The primary objective is to improve medical care quality by identifying practices with high-quality evidence and benefit-to-harm ratios.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · CLINICAL PRACTICE GUIDELINES

items.question: Which of the following best describes how clinical practice guidelines are developed?
options: Through anecdotal experiences shared among practitioners.
By using tools of Evidence-Based Medicine, including systematic literature searches and reviews.
Based solely on expert opinions without reviewing peer-reviewed literature.
By focusing exclusively on health care interventions without considering clinical disorders.
items.explanation: Guidelines are developed using the tools of EBM, including systematic literature searches and reviews.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · CLINICAL PRACTICE GUIDELINES

items.question: What is emphasized by the definition of clinical practice guidelines according to the 1990 Institute of Medicine?
options: The need for guidelines to explicitly define decisions in specific situations.
The importance of systematic development and assistance in decision-making, not defining explicit decisions.
That guidelines should replace practitioner judgment entirely.
Guidelines are only useful if they cover all possible clinical scenarios.
items.explanation: The definition emphasizes systematic development to assist decision-making, not define explicit decisions.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · CLINICAL PRACTICE GUIDELINES

items.statement: Medical practice is purely a science with no element of uncertainty.
items.explanation: The passage states that medical practice involves an intrinsic uncertainty and is not purely scientific.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · Decision-Making in Clinical Medicine

items.statement: Evidence-based medicine integrates research evidence with clinical judgment.
items.explanation: The passage describes evidence-based medicine as the integration of the best available research evidence with clinical judgment.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · Decision-Making in Clinical Medicine

items.statement: Clinical expertise is easily generalizable across different medical conditions.
items.explanation: Expertise in one area, such as hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, does not necessarily translate to better performance in unrelated areas like diagnosing neutropenia.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · BRIEF INTRODUCTION TO CLINICAL REASONING (part 1)

items.statement: Research on medical expertise is mostly centered on diagnostic reasoning.
items.explanation: The passage states that research on medical expertise remains sparse overall and is mostly focused on diagnostic reasoning.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · BRIEF INTRODUCTION TO CLINICAL REASONING (part 1)

items.statement: Pattern recognition alone is sufficient for a secure diagnosis.
items.explanation: Pattern recognition must be followed by diagnostic verification to ensure all symptoms and signs are accounted for.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · BRIEF INTRODUCTION TO CLINICAL REASONING (part 2)

items.statement: Educating physicians about cognitive biases has significantly reduced diagnostic errors.
items.explanation: There is little evidence that educating physicians on over 100 cognitive biases has affected the rate of diagnostic errors.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · BRIEF INTRODUCTION TO CLINICAL REASONING (part 2)

items.statement: The representativeness heuristic can lead to diagnostic errors by overestimating the likelihood of rare diseases.
items.explanation: Representativeness heuristic may cause clinicians to overlook a disease's low prevalence.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · BRIEF INTRODUCTION TO CLINICAL REASONING (part 3)

items.statement: The availability heuristic is based on the statistical likelihood of diseases.
items.explanation: Availability heuristic relies on recall ease, not statistical likelihood.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · BRIEF INTRODUCTION TO CLINICAL REASONING (part 3)

items.statement: Clinicians should consider aortic dissection only when typical symptoms of myocardial infarction are absent.
items.explanation: Aortic dissection should be considered routinely due to its potential fatality, even if it mimics the symptoms of more prevalent conditions like MI.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · BRIEF INTRODUCTION TO CLINICAL REASONING (part 4)

items.statement: Experts rely solely on literal recall of specific cases for diagnosis.
items.explanation: Experts use conceptual networks and illness scripts, not just literal recall, to connect symptoms and signs meaningfully.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · BRIEF INTRODUCTION TO CLINICAL REASONING (part 4)

items.subtopic: BRIEF INTRODUCTION TO CLINICAL REASONING (part 5) | PERSONALIZED DECISION-MAKING
items.statement: Personalized therapeutic decision-making involves incorporating the patient’s preferences and values for possible health outcomes.
items.explanation: Incorporating the patient's preferences and values is part of personalized therapeutic decision-making.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · BRIEF INTRODUCTION TO CLINICAL REASONING (part 5) | PERSONALIZED DECISION-MAKING

items.subtopic: BRIEF INTRODUCTION TO CLINICAL REASONING (part 5) | PERSONALIZED DECISION-MAKING
items.statement: Therapeutic effectiveness can be reliably demonstrated by observing the outcome of an individual patient.
items.explanation: Therapeutic effectiveness should be based on large groups of patients, not just individual observations.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · BRIEF INTRODUCTION TO CLINICAL REASONING (part 5) | PERSONALIZED DECISION-MAKING

items.statement: Physicians' practice styles are solely determined by published evidence and guidelines.
items.explanation: Practice styles also include beliefs about therapy effectiveness, diagnostic test use, and other influences beyond just published evidence.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · NONCLINICAL INFLUENCES ON CLINICAL DECISION-MAKING

items.statement: Physician-induced demand refers to the tendency of physicians to underuse available medical facilities.
items.explanation: Physician-induced demand describes the increased use of medical facilities and technologies once they become available.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · NONCLINICAL INFLUENCES ON CLINICAL DECISION-MAKING

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSTIC TEST PERFORMANCE: UNDERSTANDING TEST ACCURACY
items.statement: A high-sensitivity cut point is appropriate when treatment has substantial risk for harm.
items.explanation: When treatment has substantial risk, a high-specificity cut point is preferred to minimize false positives.
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · DIAGNOSTIC TEST PERFORMANCE: UNDERSTANDING TEST ACCURACY

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSTIC TEST PERFORMANCE: UNDERSTANDING TEST ACCURACY
items.statement: The ROC curve plots sensitivity against 1 - specificity.
items.explanation: An ROC curve displays the trade-off between sensitivity and specificity by plotting sensitivity (y-axis) versus 1-specificity (x-axis).
items.reference: Decision Making in Clinical Medicine · DIAGNOSTIC TEST PERFORMANCE: UNDERSTANDING TEST ACCURACY

items.subtopic: MEASURES OF DISEASE PROBABILITY AND BAYES' RULE
items.statement: Predictive value is an intrinsic measure of test accuracy.
